front: What is the purpose of a truth table in propositional logic?
back: A truth table is used to determine the truth value of a compound proposition for all possible truth values of its constituent simple propositions. It systematically lists all combinations of inputs (T/F) and the resulting output of the logical expression.

front: Define the logical connective 'conjunction' (AND) and provide its symbol.
back: Conjunction is a compound proposition that is true only when both of its constituent propositions are true. It is denoted by the symbol ∧. For propositions p and q, p ∧ q is the conjunction.

front: What is the truth table for the conditional (implication) connective p → q?
back:
p → q is false only when p is true and q is false. It is true in all other cases:

p | q | p → q
T | T |   T
T | F |   F
F | T |   T
F | F |   T

front: What is a tautology? Provide a brief definition.
back: A tautology is a compound proposition that is always true, regardless of the truth values of the constituent propositions that form it. Its truth table column contains only 'T' (true) values.

front: Explain the exclusive OR (XOR) connective and how it differs from the inclusive OR.
back: The exclusive OR, denoted by ⊕ or XOR, is a compound proposition that is true only when exactly one of p or q is true (i.e., p or q is true, but not both). This differs from the inclusive OR (∨), which is true when at least one is true, including the case where both are true.
